headline: Malaysia's Semiconductor Packaging Revenue Hits RM120B
context: MIDA reported that Malaysia's semiconductor packaging, assembly, and testing (OSAT) sector generated RM120 billion in revenue in 2025, making Malaysia the world's 6th largest chip packaging hub, though value-capture remains at the lower end of the semiconductor chain.

cards.big: RM120 billion semiconductor revenue — but Malaysia does packaging, not design, capturing only 8% of chip value chain
cards.sub: Intel, Infineon, and Texas Instruments run factories here — Malaysian companies own less than 12% of sector output

cards.big: OSAT margins average 12-15% versus chip design margins of 45-65% — Malaysia is in the low-profit segment of a high-value industry
cards.sub: Moving up to wafer fabrication requires RM40-80 billion investment per fab — beyond Malaysia's current capital capacity

cards.big: Semiconductor sector employs 580,000 workers but only 22% are in engineering roles — 78% in production line assembly
cards.sub: Average production worker earns RM2,200 monthly while engineers earn RM5,800 — but engineering positions grew only 4% vs 18% for assembly

cards.big: MIDA's semiconductor incentive packages offer 15-year tax holidays but lack mandatory technology transfer requirements
cards.sub: After 50 years of hosting multinational chip operations, Malaysia still cannot independently design a commercial-grade semiconductor

cards.big: Being the world's 6th largest chip packager sounds impressive — until you realise packaging captures the smallest share of the value
Should Malaysia's next wave of chip incentives require multinationals to establish local R&D centres and train 5,000 design engineers by 2030?
